coredata.dc:title: Color-switchable hybrid dots/hydroxyethyl cellulose ink for anti-counterfeiting applications
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Xijun
Li, Guoqing
Cai, Shaoyong
Yang, Heng
Lin, Kang
He, Miao
Wen, Junwei
Li, Houbin
Xiong, Yabo
Chen, Dongzhi
Liu, Xinghai
coredata.dc:description: Many anti-counterfeiting inks have been explored recently, most of them are commonly involved in weak fastness, high cost and long-term toxicity, impeding their real-life applications. Herein, an environment-friendly and inexpensive anti-counterfeiting ink with excellent fastness is reported. The untifake ink is developed by combining hybrid dots (silicon/carbon) with hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) binder. Interestingly, the HEC binder can effectively prevent from aggregation-induced quenching of hybrid dots. Subsequently, the customized patterns are successfully transferred onto different surfaces of various substrates including cotton fabric, cellulosic paper, glass, metal, silicon wafer and PET film, using the as-prepared ink by screen-printing technique, exhibiting that the hybrid dots/HEC ink possesses widespread practicability. Notably, fluorescent color of these patterns can be switchable by adjusting environmental pH-value, further imparting the as-prepared ink with excellent covert performance. This new fluorescent hybrid dots/HEC ink will be promising candidates for high-level anti-counterfeiting applications including food packaging, apparel and documents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anti-counterfeiting ink
Hybrid dot
Hydroxyethyl cellulose
Optical property
Fastness

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the fluorescent hybrid dots/HEC inks
Preparation of the printed samples with customized patterns
pH-responsive experiments of the printed patterns
Compositions and structure characterizations of the hybrid dots
Optical properties of the hybrid dots
Rheological properties of the hybrid dots/HEC inks
The anti-counterfeiting applications of the hybrid dots/HEC inks
Fastness of the representative patterns printed on different substrates

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research has received financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 51503161 and 51776143 ), Scientific Research Foundation of Hubei Provincial Department of Education (grant number D20191702 ), the Open Project Program of High-Tech Organic Fibers Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (grant number 20200518-1 ), Wuhan Textile University Training Program of Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship (grant number S202010495001 and S202010495003 ) and Wuhan University Training Program of Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship (grant number S2019217005 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrations of the preparative process of the ink of hybrid dots and hybrid dots/HEC for anti-counterfeiting applications.
Morphology of the hybrid dots: a) TEM images of the hybrid dots, the inset is size distribution of particles and b) the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of the hybrid dots.
FTIR and XPS spectra of the hybrid dots: a) FTIR spectrum, b) full XPS spectrum and c-f) high-resolution XPS peaks of the C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Si 2p.

a) UV–vis absorption and transmittance spectra of the hybrid dots, the inset is photographs of the hybrid dot dispersion under daylight (Left) and 365 nm UV light (Right), b) PL peak intensity versus excitation wavelength, c) the effect of pH value on the fluorescence intensity of the hybrid dot dispersion under 365 nm UV excitation and d) the CIE chromaticity diagram with emission color coordinates for the hybrid dots under different pH-values.

Rheological properties of the hybrid dots/HEC inks: a) the relationship between viscosity and the shear rate of the hybrid inks and b) shear viscosity versus time at three different shear rates (0.1 s−1 for 30 s, 1000 s−1 for another 30 s, and 0.1 s−1 for 10 min).

The photograph of the various samples with patterns printed on a variety of substrates under daylight and 365 nm UV illumination: a) hybrid dots/HEC ink for screen-printing, b) cotton fabric, c) cellulosic paper, d) glass, e) stainless steel, f) silicon wafer, g) PET film, h) fluorescent ink for inkjet-printing and i) QR code of Wuhan University inkjet printed on cellulosic paper.

The photograph of the printed patterns on different substrates under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light after treatment with hydrochloric gas and ammonia vapor for different times: Wuhan University emblem on cotton fabric (over) and number “100” on cellulosic paper (under).

Fastness experimental pictures of the QR code of Wuhan University screen-printed on representative substrates under the 365 nm UV illumination: a) cotton fabric rubbed with 2B eraser, b) cotton fabric after tape stripping, c) dry cotton fabric rubbed with abrasive paper, d) wetted cotton fabric rubbed with abrasive paper, e) cellulosic paper without rubbing, f) cellulosic paper rubbed with 2B eraser, g) cellulosic paper after tape stripping, h) wetted cellulosic paper rubbed abrasive paper, effect of rubbing times on photoluminescent intensity of the QR code of Wuhan University printed on i) cotton fabric and j) cellulosic paper.
The formulations of the fluorescent inks of hybrid dots.

head.ce:title: Color-switchable hybrid dots/hydroxyethyl cellulose ink for anti-counterfeiting applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan, 430200, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Printing and Packaging, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Printing and Packaging, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Textile Materials & Advanced Processing Technology, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430073, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Textile Materials & Advanced Processing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Textile Materials & Advanced Processing Technology, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430073, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan, 430200, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Many anti-counterfeiting inks have been explored recently, most of them are commonly involved in weak fastness, high cost and long-term toxicity, impeding their real-life applications. Herein, an environment-friendly and inexpensive anti-counterfeiting ink with excellent fastness is reported. The untifake ink is developed by combining hybrid dots (silicon/carbon) with hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) binder. Interestingly, the HEC binder can effectively prevent from aggregation-induced quenching of hybrid dots. Subsequently, the customized patterns are successfully transferred onto different surfaces of various substrates including cotton fabric, cellulosic paper, glass, metal, silicon wafer and PET film, using the as-prepared ink by screen-printing technique, exhibiting that the hybrid dots/HEC ink possesses widespread practicability. Notably, fluorescent color of these patterns can be switchable by adjusting environmental pH-value, further imparting the as-prepared ink with excellent covert performance. This new fluorescent hybrid dots/HEC ink will be promising candidates for high-level anti-counterfeiting applications including food packaging, apparel and documents.

ce:section.ce:para: Counterfeiting is a global problem that causes a significant negative impact among governments, enterprises and consumers (Andres et al., 2014; Fang et al., 2019; Skwierczyńska et al., 2019). Over the past decades, forgeries have been found everywhere, such as the fast-moving consumer goods, pharmaceutical, microelectronics, automotive parts and value documents (Andres et al., 2014). To fight against forgery, diverse anti-counterfeiting techniques have been developed, such as QR codes (Han et al., 2012; Li et al., 2017), radio frequency identification tags with electronic tracking function (Li et al., 2017; Yao et al., 2017), laser holography (Donaldson, 2019; Li et al., 2018) and plasmonic labels (Cui et al., 2014). Unfortunately, the anti-counterfeiting techniques mentioned above are easily imitated with rapid advancement of technology. Thus, persistent efforts should be devoted to exploring advanced anti-counterfeiting technologies with low cost and high-level security to deter for forgeries.

Recently, a variety of fluorescent materials have triggered widespread interests in the field of anti-counterfeiting inks, such as organic dyes/pigments (Abdollahi et al., 2018a, 2018b; Chen, Hu et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2018; Lafleur et al., 2019; Yin et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2014), polymer dots (Yang, Ho et al., 2019), rare-earth materials (Li et al., 2017; Liang et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2017; Yao et al., 2019; Zhang, Chen et al., 2017), and semiconductor quantum dots, (Bao et al., 2015; Han et al., 2017; Kumar et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2019; Yang Liu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018). However, there are still some common concerns on the limited photostability of organic dyes/pigments, tedious time-consuming synthetic steps of the polymer dots, high cost of rare-earth particles and long-term toxicity of conventional quantum dots, respectively, (Chen, Cui et al., 2019; Song et al., 2016; Yang, Ho et al., 2019). To date, carbon and silicon quantum dots have attracted extensive attention owing to their unique advantages including nontoxicity, excellent photoluminescent properties, superior biocompatibility and high abundance in nature (Campos et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2017; Cuevas et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2017; Peng et al., 2014; Qu et al., 2012; Shirahata et al., 2010). But research interests of the two fluorescent nanomaterials mainly focus on bio-imaging, drug delivery and chemical sensors (Lv et al., 2017; Zhang & Yu, 2014). Only carbon dots have been developed and used for water-dispersible anticounterfeiting inks (Atchudan et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2017; Pang et al., 2018; Qu et al., 2012; Song et al., 2016). However, the current anti-counterfeiting applications of the carbon dots only rely on the fluorescence in single excitation mode of UV light. Meanwhile, the fluorescence is usually quenched when the inks of the carbon dots are deposited on glass, metal, silicon, and plastic substrates due to aggregation. Moreover, the water-dispersed inks based on carbon dots are easily washed off with water (Qu et al., 2012). Therefore, it is very promising to develop nontoxic water-dispersed fluorescent inks based on silicon and carbon dots with more covert, reliable and durable features for high-level anti-counterfeiting applications.

In theory, water-soluble polymer can prevent from fluorescence quenching and improve fastness when introduced into the water-dispersible fluorescent inks as binder. It is well-established that hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC), as one of the most important water-soluble cellulose derivatives, has been extensively applied to many industrial fields as a stabilizer, thickener, coating, pharmaceutical and cosmetic due to the striking advantages of high biocompatibility, low toxicity and excellent film-forming properties (Wen et al., 2018). Therefore, it is promising to formulate new water-dispersible inks by the combination of advantages of HEC and carbon/silicon dots. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first time for us to report the water-dispersible inks based on hybrid dots (silicon/carbon) and HEC for anti-counterfeiting applications. In this work, an environment-friendly, cheap and durable water-dispersible anti-counterfeiting ink with excellent fastness was formulated by combining the merits of hybrid dots and HEC. Subsequently, some customized patterns were successfully deposited onto surfaces of different substrates such as cotton fabric, cellulosic paper, glass, metal, silicon wafer and PET film using the as-prepared ink by screen-printing technique, and their concealing performance and practicability were examined and discussed.

3-(2-Aminoethylamino) propyl-dimethoxymethylsilane (CG-602, 99.9 %) was purchased from Nanjing Chengong Organic Silicon Material Co., Ltd. Sodium citrate (98 %), hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC, 1000∼1500 mPa.s, 25 °C) and quinine sulfate (98 %) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Potassium bromide (AR), hydrochloric acid (AR) and ammonia solution (27 %) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off: 1000 Da) and microporous filtration membrane (0.22 um, Φ = 50 mm) were purchased from Spectrumlabs and Shanghai Xingya Purification Material Factory, respectively. Desized and scoured cotton fabrics (200 g/m2), stainless steel plate, silicon wafer and polyethylene terephthalate (PET) film and cellulosic paper were available in local suppliers. Deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ) was used in the following experiments.

Dispersion of hybrid dots was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal reaction according to the well-established literature (Zhang & Yu, 2014). The resulting dispersion of hybrid dots was further filtered with microporous membrane to remove some impurities, and its solid content was less than 6.35 %. Afterward, the filtered solution was dialyzed against DI water for 2 days to remove small molecules. Finally, a faint yellow solid (Figure S1) was obtained by freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the fluorescent hybrid dots/HEC inks
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, different mass of HEC (from 0.2 to 0.5 g) was charged into DI water, and magnetically stirred for 12 h to afford HEC solutions. Then, 1.0 g dispersion of hybrid dots filtered by microporous membrane was added into the above HEC solutions, and stirred uniformly to formulate the fluorescent hybrid dots/HEC inks. According to the mass ratio of HEC in ink, these fluorescent inks are denoted as FI-2 (Fluorescent-ink-2%), FI-3, FI-4 and FI-5, respectively. The detailed recipes of the inks are listed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the printed samples with customized patterns
ce:section.ce:para: To check feasibility for security application, the customized patterns such as logo and QR code of Wuhan University were printed on cotton fabric using the formulated-above ink, cellulosic paper, glass, stainless steel, silicon wafer and PET film, by screen-printing (250 mesh-counts), respectively. Before printing, only PET substrate needs to be pretreated by washing several times with water and ethanol, and drying at room temperature. For a comparison, the ink of hybrid dots for ink-jet printing without HEC binder was also prepared by re-dispersing the powder of hybrid dots (20 mg) into DI water (10 mL), which was denoted as FI-0. Meanwhile, the cellulosic paper was chosen as the substrate for inkjet printing due to uniform smoothness and softness. QR code were printed on the cellulosic paper by the HP inkjet-2050. After printing, all these substrates were dried at 100 °C for 30 min under vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-responsive experiments of the printed patterns
ce:section.ce:para: Cotton textile printed with the emblem of Wuhan University and cellulosic paper printed with the characters “100” were used during pH-responsive experiment. Concentrated hydrochloric acid and concentrated ammonia solution were poured into two staining jars to produce acidic and alkaline environments, respectively. The detailed operation of the pH-responsive experiment is as follows: firstly, the printings were placed in the vapor atmosphere of hydrochloric gas for about 30 s to ensure full absorption of hydrochloric gas. Subsequently, the above samples were also placed in ammonia atmosphere for another 30 s. The entire process mentioned-above was denoted as one cycle. And then, the above steps were repeated several times to evaluate pH-responsive reversibility of the fluorescent patterns. During the whole process, the color changes of these patterns after UV light illumination were recorded by a digital camera.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a JEOL-2100 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) under an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out on a PANalytical Empyrean Series 2 instrument equipped with Cu Ka radiation, and a step size and scanning rate were 0.02° and 5°/min, respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained on a Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, Waltham, MA) over the range of 4000 to 400 cm−1 at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations were performed on an ESCALAB250Xi electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded on a UV-3600 optical spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra of samples were collected on an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with an external CNI (2 W) 980 nm laser system. Time-resolved PL decay were recorded on an Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrophotometer. The rheological behavior of the hybrid dot fluorescent inks was studied by the rotary rheometer (Kinexus Pro+, Malvern Instruments Ltd, Britain) equipped with a 60 mm cone-plate system with a cone angle of 1° and gap of 1 mm to estimate the printability of fluorescent ink on different substrates. The adhesion and abrasion tests of the patterns printed on cotton textile and non-background paper were performed by use of the 3 M adhesive tape (# 600) and rubber eraser according to ASTM D-3359 standard, respectively. Additionally, the dry and wet fastness of the patterns printed on cotton textile and cellulosic paper were also evaluated by changes in the fluorescent intensity of the rubbed patterns according to EN ISO 105-X12 standard. The general test procedure is described as follows. During rubbing, sand paper (1200 mesh) was used as an abrasive surface, the pattern printed on the representative cotton fabric was placed faced-down onto sand paper under loading of 500 g and dragged sample to move to and fro in a straight line along with a 10.4 cm track. One back and forth defines a time. (Mai et al., 2019).

Scheme 1 shows the manufacturing processes of the inks of hybrid dots/HEC and their anti-counterfeiting applications. Firstly, hybrid dots were synthesized through one-step hydrothermal reaction of CG-602 with sodium citrate at 160 °C for 12 h. Then, the hybrid dot inks suitable for screen-printing on a variety of substrates and inkjet printing on cellulosic paper were prepared, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Compositions and structure characterizations of the hybrid dots
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 a, near-spherical particles with a size of 2.99 ± 0.65 nm are clearly observed. The HR-TEM image shows that two types of particles are found. As shown in Fig. 1b, a lattice spacing of 0.19 nm is clearly observed, which is attributed to the (220) planes of diamond silicon (Lee et al., 2015). In the meantime, another lattice spacings of 0.21 nm and 0.24 nm are also found, which are attributable to the (100) and the (1121) lattice planes of graphitic carbon, respectively (Far’ain Md Noor, Saiful Badri, Salleh, & Umar, 2018; Tian et al., 2017; Yang, Liu et al., 2019). Subsequently, chemical composition of the hybrid dots was also characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD). As displayed in Figure S2, the XRD pattern of the hybrid dots shows a broad peak at 22° and a small peak at 46°, which corresponds to the (002) lattice plane of an amorphous graphitic carbon (Liu et al., 2017), and the (220) lattice plane of a silicon particle (Chen et al., 2016), respectively, indicating that the resultant hybrid dots are composed of the silicon and carbon particles.

Subsequently, chemical compositions of the hybrid dots have also been characterized by FTIR spectroscopy. The peaks located at around 1420 and 2977 cm−1 are ascribed to the bending vibration and stretching vibration of C–H bonds from the surface of the hybrid dots, respectively (Gong et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2015; Zhang & Yu, 2014). Notably, a strong peak located at 1083 cm−1 is assigned to the SiOSi stretching vibration (Shirahata et al., 2010), indicating that hydrolytic condensation of alkoxy groups from the silane molecules under hydrothermal condition occurred. The evidently strong absorption peak at 1592 cm−1 is attributable to the bending vibration of the NH bond (Zhang & Yu, 2014; Zhong et al., 2013). The peak at 3415 cm−1 should be ascribed to the overlapping absorptions of the NH and the OH stretching vibrations, in which the OH groups are the result of hydrolysis of the methoxy groups attached to the silicon atoms (Gong et al., 2019; Zhang & Yu, 2014).

To further characterize the chemical compositions of the fabricated hybrid dots, XPS measurements were performed. Four peaks are present at 284.36, 399.59, 531.08 and 101.42 eV, corroborating that the as-prepared hybrid dots consist of C (54.76 %), N (4.35 %), O (35.36 %) and Si (5.53 %) elements, as exhibited in Fig. 2 b. Meanwhile, Fig. 2c presents that the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s band can be fit into three peaks at 284.79, 286.43 and 288.57 eV, which are ascribed to CC/CN, CO and CO, respectively (Datta et al., 2016; Zan et al., 2018; Zhang, Chang et al., 2017). The high-resolution N 1s spectrum in Fig. 2d exhibits two peaks at 398.95 and 400.04 eV, corresponding to CNC and NH bond, respectively (Gong et al., 2019; Zan et al., 2018). Two peaks associated to 531.51 eV for CO (Zhan et al., 2018) and 532.02 eV for CO (Guo et al., 2018) indicate that the oxygen-containing groups are also present on surface of the hybrid dots, as shown in Fig. 2e. The Si 2p band (Fig. 2f) at 102.1 eV is attributed to the formation of SiOSi (Wu et al., 2015), which is consistent with the results of FTIR. Collectively, OH, amino and amide groups available on the surface of the hybrid dots can make it easily disperse in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the hybrid dots
ce:section.ce:para: The yellowish dispersion of hybrid dots under daylight exhibits bright blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light. A wide absorption band at around 355 nm is observed, which could be ascribed to the n-π∗ transition CO or COH on the surface of hybrid dots (Liu et al., 2015; Luo et al., 2018). At the same time, the transmittance of the dispersion of hybrid dots under visible light exceeds up to 90 %, exhibiting excellent transparency, as shown in Fig. 3 a. It is noteworthy that the PL emission intensity of hybrid dots increases with the increasing the excitation wavelength from 325 to 375 nm, and then decreases gradually with a further increase in the excitation wavelength. While the maximum emission peak centered at about 447 nm cannot shift with the increment of excitation wavelength, which indicates the excitation-independent fluorescent behavior of the hybrid dots due to surface state of the hybrid dots, as shown in Figs. 3b and S3. Afterward, the influence of pH-value on the fluorescence of the hybrid dots was also studied. It is interesting to find that with a decrease of pH-value, the fluorescence intensity decreases gradually, proving that the dispersion of hybrid dots is pH-responsive, as shown in Fig. 3c. This pH-responsive phenomenon may be due to the protonation of amino groups on the surface of hybrid dots, which enhances the intramolecular electron transfer ability between –NH3 + and hybrid dots and resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity (Guo et al., 2018; Zhang & Yu, 2014). Meanwhile, the maximum emission peak on PL spectra of the dispersion of hybrid dots shifts from 444 to 470 nm with the decrease of pH value (from 10 to 2), corroborating that a red-shift phenomenon occurs. Moreover, with the increase of the pH value from 2 to 10, the fixed coordinates of the fluorescence of the hybrid dots marked on the CIE chromaticity diagram also shift from cyan (0.16, 0.24) to deep-blue (0.15, 0.10), respectively, indicating that the hybrid ink possesses color-switchable fluorescence by changing pH-value, as exhibited in Fig. 3d. The color-switchable optical property also indicates that hybrid dots have potential application for anti-counterfeiting.

Additionally, the relative quantum yield of the hybrid dots was calculated to be 39 % according to literature method (Wei et al., 2014), as shown in Figure S4. The PL lifetime of hybrid dots in different pH-value environment were further measured, as shown in Figure S5. Obviously, the lifetime of the hybrid dots was shortened from 14.68 to 9.85 ns with the decrease of pH valve (from 10 to 2), which could be ascribed to the quenching mechanism that the protonation of amino groups on the surface of hybrid dots enhances the intramolecular electron transfer between –NH3 + and hybrid dots and results in a short in fluorescence lifetime.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rheological properties of the hybrid dots/HEC inks
ce:section.ce:para: It is well-known that the rheological behavior of ink plays a vital role in screen-printing (Chen, Hu et al., 2019; Faddoul et al., 2012; Liang et al., 2016). Therefore, the rheological properties of the fluorescent hybrid dots/HEC inks were studied. As displayed in Fig. 4 a, the viscosity of all the fluorescent inks decreased rapidly with the increase of shear rate ranging from 0.1–1000 s−1, as a result of the H-bond destruction of the HEC network. The shear-thinning behavior indicates that the fluorescent inks were pseudo‐plastic fluid, which is conducive to the smooth transfer of ink during the printing process. Subsequently, peak hold step test consisting of holding the sample at different shear rates in three interval times was used to simulate screen-printing process to further investigate how the HEC content affects the thixotropic behavior of fluorescent ink (Liang et al., 2016; Wen et al., 2018). In the first step, the shear rate was set at 0.1 s−1 for 30 s to examine the initial state of the fluorescent inks before screen-printing. And then, the shear rate was increased to 1000 s−1 and kept for another 30 s to simulate the squeezed process during screen-printing. The shear rate was finally reduced to 0.1 s−1 and maintained for 10 min to observe the viscosity recovery after the shear was withdrawn. As shown in Fig. 4b, the initial viscosities of the FI-2, FI-3, FI-4 and FI-5 are 1.16, 6.32, 19.22 and 58.31 Pa·s at the shear rate of 0.1 s-1, respectively, conforming that a higher content of HEC endows the ink with greater viscosity. As the content of HEC is 5 wt%, the ink exhibits too high viscosity, which would bring about difficulty in fluidity, and cause mesh blockage during printing. On the contrary, FI-2 with much lower viscosity likely contributes to the sedimentation of fluorescent particles and the diffusion of the printed traces, resulting in edge blurring of the printed pattern (Chen, Hu et al., 2019; Tian et al., 2019). The viscosities of all the inks drop sharply when the shear rate increased to 1000 s−1, which indicates the fluorescent inks can smoothly pass through the screen holes and facilitate the transfer of ink during printing process. When the shear rate decreased from 1000 s−1 to 0.1 s−1, the gel network of fluorescent inks is reconstructed, which makes the viscosity of all the inks recover to nearly 100 % of the original value and the recovery time are 54, 84, 116 and 124 s, respectively. Thus, FI-4 also has enough time to level for getting fine patterns (Chen, Hu et al., 2019; Tian et al., 2019). In comparison, FI-4 exhibits the most suitable viscosity and excellent thixotropic behavior for screen-printing process. Hence, FI-4 was chosen as the optimum formula.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The anti-counterfeiting applications of the hybrid dots/HEC inks
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 a clearly shows that the hybrid dots/HEC ink is colorless under daylight, it can emit strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV illumination. Subsequently, to examine practicability of the as-prepared ink, the customized patterns (logo and QR code of Wuhan University and orchid grass) were deposited onto surfaces of a variety of substrates such as cotton fabric, cellulosic paper, glass, stainless steel plate, silicon wafer and PET film by screen-printing technique, respectively. It is noteworthy that no printed pattern has been observed on the surfaces of cotton fabric, cellulosic paper, glass and PET film under daylight except mirror surfaces (stainless steel plate and silicon wafer). Fortunately, the logo and QR code of Wuhan University and orchid grass printed on the above substrates display strong blue fluorescence and are visible under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light, indicating that the hybrid dots/HEC ink possesses excellent practicability on the various substrates. In the meantime, the blue fluorescent patterns printed on cotton fabric, cellulosic paper, glass, stainless steel plate, silicon wafer and PET film further corroborate that the HEC binder can successfully be used as a stabilizer to inhibit aggregation-induced quenching of carbon dots, as exhibited in Fig. 5b–g. Upon turning off the UV lamp, the blue patterns screen-printed on these substrates disappear again, indicating that the hybrid dots/HEC ink has primary hidden performance.

For a comparison, the ink of hybrid dots (FI-0) suitable for inkjet-printing was also prepared. The QR code of Wuhan University was inkjet-printed on cellulosic paper. The inkjet-printed QR code is also invisible by the naked eye under daylight, and a blue QR code with clear textures can be readable when exposed to UV light illumination, as demonstrated in Fig. 5h–i. Furthermore, the hidden information can be read out well with the aid of QR code recognizer or smartphone, thus realizing double anti-counterfeiting protection.

Because of the excellent pH-responsive optical property of hybrid dots, the color-switch of the patterns printed on the representative substrates was examined in different pH-value. As mentioned above, the printed cotton fabric and cellulosic paper show the blue fluorescent patterns of Wuhan University emblem and the characters “100” respectively under UV light. Interestingly, the fluorescent color of the patterns changes to cyan quickly when exposed in hydrochloric gas atmosphere. Subsequently, the above patterns are placed in an ammonia gas atmosphere, and the color of fluorescent patterns quickly recovers to blue, which indicates that the fluorescent color of these patterns is switchable. As shown in Fig. 6 , after being repeated to 30 times, the fluorescent color of these patterns can still be quickly and reversibly converted only by adjusting the environmental pH-value. This color-switchable fluorescent behavior endows the as-prepared hybrid dots/HEC inks with excellent concealing performance, expecting that they will be applied to high-level anti-counterfeiting fields in the future, such as food packaging, apparel, banknotes, passport, diplomas and certificates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fastness of the representative patterns printed on different substrates
ce:section.ce:para: Unfortunately, only the pattern printed on PET film can be wiped off due to weak physical interaction between HEC binder and PET. To examine fastness of the hybrid dots/HEC ink, the fluorescent intensity of the select pattern (the QR code of Wuhan University) screen-printed on the representative substrates (cotton textile and cellulosic paper) were evaluated by different methods such as rubber test, abrasion resistance and tape stripping, respectively. Fig. 7 a-h exhibits that complete fluorescent patterns are still discernible on the surfaces of the cotton fabric and cellulosic paper under 365 nm irradiation after being treated with a tape peeling, rubber rubbing and abrasive paper, respectively. The undamaged fluorescent patterns printed on cellulose-based substrates corroborate that the hybrid dots/HEC inks possess impressive fastness on those substrates, which should be ascribed to hydroxy groups and amino groups available in the inks consisting of hybrid dots and hydroxyethyl cellulose. Meanwhile, the dry and wet fastness of the as-prepared hybrid dots/HEC inks were quantitively evaluated through measurement of changes in the fluorescent intensity of select QR code of Wuhan University printed on cotton textile and cellulosic paper before and after treatment. The fluorescent intensity of the printed pattern on the dry cotton fabric slightly reduces from 0.48 ± 0.06 to 0.41 ± 0.02 as the printed pattern was rubbed with 2B eraser from 0 to 100 times. Similarly, the fluorescent intensity of the printed pattern also slightly decreases to 0.34 ± 0.03 and 0.31 ± 0.01 after the dry cotton fabrics were treated with tape stripping 100 times and rubbed with abrasive paper 100 times, respectively. These reducing fluorescent intensity further confirms that both tape stripping and abrasive paper rubbing are greater destructive to the hybrid dots/HEC ink as compared with rubber test. The decreasing fluorescent intensity still maintains up to 0.31 ± 0.01 even though the dry pattern on cotton fabric was rubbed with abrasive paper 100 times, indicating that the hybrid dots/HEC ink has excellent dry fastness. Subsequently, no marked changes are observed in the fluorescent intensity of the pattern after the screen-printed cotton fabric was wetted and rubbed with abrasive paper 100 times, proving that the hybrid dots/HEC ink has excellent wet fastness, as exhibited in Fig. 7a–d and i. The fastness of hybrid dots/HEC ink printed on cellulosic paper is also examined. After the patterns printed on cellulosic paper were treated with eraser rubbing, tape stripping and abrasive paper rubbing, respectively, their changes in the fluorescent intensity show the same trend as compared to the pattern printed on cotton fabric. But the pattern on the cellulosic paper cannot be subjected to tape stripping or wetly rubbing with abrasive paper up to 50 times because further tape stripping and rubbing with abrasive paper are prone to damage the cellulosic paper substrate. Notably, the fluorescent intensity of the rubbed pattern still remains up to 0.18 ± 0.02 even though the screen-printed cellulosic paper was treated with tape stripping 50 times, exhibiting that hybrid dots/HEC ink printed on cellulosic paper has excellent dry fastness, as shown in Fig. 7j. In the meantime, Fig. 7j also displays that the wetted pattern printed on cellulosic paper has an evident fluorescent intensity of 0.14 ± 0.03 after being rubbed with abrasive paper 50 times, implying that the hybrid dots/HEC ink has good wet fastness to abrasive paper. Collectively, the excellent dry and wet fastness further demonstrates that the HEC binder endows hybrid dots/HEC ink with universal practicability.

In summary, water-dispersible hybrid dots with sensitive pH-responsive optical property were synthesized via one-step hydrothermal method. Subsequently, novel water-based anti-counterfeiting inks with excellent thixotropic properties and strong fastness were explored by the combination of the advantages of the hybrid dots and hydroxyethyl cellulose. Subsequently, various customized patterns were deposited onto different flexible substrates including cotton fabric, cellulosic paper, glass, metal, silicon wafer and PET film, using the as-prepared inks by screen-printing, exhibiting that the hybrid dots/HEC ink possesses widespread practicability. Interestingly, the HEC binder can effectively prevent from aggregation-induced quenching of carbon dots. Notably, the fluorescent color of these patterns can be reversibly switched just by adjusting the pH-value. The low-cost, environment-friendly and color-switchable fluorescent hydroxyethyl cellulose/hybrid dot inks will be potential candidates for high-level anti-counterfeiting including food packaging, apparel, banknotes and confidential documents. This work will open a new avenue for developing advanced anti-counterfeiting inks based on hydroxyethyl cellulose.

Xijun Fu: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft. Guoqing Li: Methodology, Resources. Shaoyong Cai: Visualization, Investigation. Heng Yang: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Kang Lin: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Miao He: Software, Validation. Junwei Wen: Investigation, Methodology. Houbin Li: Supervision. Yabo Xiong: Writing - review & editing. Dongzhi Chen: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Xinghai Liu: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research has received financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 51503161 and 51776143), Scientific Research Foundation of Hubei Provincial Department of Education (grant number D20191702), the Open Project Program of High-Tech Organic Fibers Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (grant number 20200518-1), Wuhan Textile University Training Program of Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship (grant number S202010495001 and S202010495003) and Wuhan University Training Program of Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship (grant number S2019217005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2020.117084.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
